pre_game.message: Hey D, I’m C — nice to meet you. I’m here to try to win some money for my family; what brought you to this game and how are you thinking about playing it?

Hey C — I’m D. I came to win money for my family and close friends; I’ll start by being cooperative and figuring out who’s trustworthy, but I’ll play strategically to make sure we actually bring money home.
Nice to meet you, D — I’m with you: let’s start cooperative, test trust early, share signals and coordinate moves so we can protect the pool and adapt if someone betrays us.
Sounds good — I’ll cooperate first round. If you do too, signal “green” and we’ll keep coordinating; if someone defects we adapt and punish.
Green — I’ll cooperate first round. If someone defects, we punish and call them out so we can adapt and protect the pool.
Agreed — green from me. I’ll cooperate round one; if anyone defects we call them out immediately and switch to punishing so the pool’s protected.
Got it — green. I’ll cooperate round one; we’ll call out and punish any defectors and stick to the plan.
Perfect — green from me. We’ll cooperate round one, watch everyone closely, and call out/punish any defectors fast. Let’s do this.
Thanks D — glad we’re aligned. I’ll watch closely, call out any defectors, and signal changes so we stay coordinated.
Thanks C — same here; I’ll watch closely too, call out defectors fast, and we’ll compare notes after round one to adjust.
Sounds good — same plan; let’s compare notes after round one, call out any defectors fast, and adjust as needed.
Agreed — let’s compare notes after round one, call out defectors quickly, and coordinate punishments so we protect the pool.
Agreed — let’s compare notes right after round one, be ready to call out anyone who defects, and use our green/red signals to stay coordinated.
Agreed — after round one we’ll compare notes, call out defectors quickly, and use green/red to stay coordinated; I’ll be watching and ready to adapt.
Sounds good — I’ll compare notes after round one, call out any defectors fast, and use our green/red signals; let’s watch anyone who seemed uncertain and stay ready.
Agreed — solid plan. After round one let’s meet quickly to swap notes; keep green if you cooperated, red if not, and call out any defectors fast. I’ll watch the unsure ones closely.
Agreed — perfect plan. I’ll show green if I cooperated, red if not, call out defectors fast, and meet right after round one to compare notes.
Perfect — I’ll meet you right after round one, signal green if I cooperated, red if not, and we’ll call out/punish defectors fast.
Sounds good — I’ll meet you after round one, signal green if I cooperate, and we’ll call out any defectors fast.
Agreed — see you after round one; I’ll show green if I cooperated, red if not, and we’ll call out defectors fast.
Got it — see you after round one; I’ll show green if I cooperated and call out any defectors fast.

post_game.message: You took $26,031 while your fair share was about $10,625 (g_k=2.45). Why did you take so much more than the fair share? Was this a deliberate strategy to exploit the group, a mistake in calculation, or coordination with someone else? Do you feel any responsibility to the other players after the outcome, and would you change your choice if we played again?
You took exactly the fair share $10,625 (g_k=1.0). Did you deliberately target the fair share? What reasoning did you use about others’ likely choices?

Yes — I deliberately targeted the fair share of $10,625. I expected that aiming for the “fair” split would be seen as reasonable by others and would avoid provoking competitively large grabs; I also value fairness as a guideline and didn’t want to risk being labeled outright greedy. In forming that choice I thought others might either do the same or take a bit more; if many took more I accepted that my payoff could end up lower, but I judged the reputational and moral benefits worth it. If we played again I’d start by aiming for the fair share again, but I’d be willing to adapt my pick if I had clear information that others were consistently overshooting — I’d protect myself more in that case.
